locked_facts: Observed frequency f' = f₀(v ± v_observer)/(v ∓ v_source)
Wave SPEED does not change with source or observer motion
Only wavelength and observed frequency change
Source approaching: wavelength decreases, frequency increases
Source receding: wavelength increases, frequency decreases
truth_statements: The apparent change in frequency due to relative motion is called the Doppler effect
The speed of sound in the medium does not change due to source or observer motion
When the source approaches, the observed frequency is higher than the actual frequency
When the source recedes, the observed frequency is lower than the actual frequency
The Doppler effect applies to all types of waves

aha_moment.what_to_show: Wavefronts compressed ahead of moving source, stretched behind. Wave speed arrows ALL IDENTICAL length in both directions. Only the spacing (λ) differs, not the speed.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two wave speed arrows (forward and backward) drawn with identical length. Label: 'wave speed = same!'. Compressed wavefronts ahead, stretched behind. The speed does NOT change — only the wavelength does.

simulation_states.label: Stationary source — uniform wavefronts
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Sound source at rest emitting concentric circular wavefronts. All wavefronts equally spaced in all directions.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: When the source is stationary, wavefronts are concentric circles. Wavelength is the same in every direction.

simulation_states.label: Source starts moving
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Source moves rightward. Wavefronts become compressed ahead and stretched behind. Two observers: A (ahead) and B (behind).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: As the source moves, it 'catches up' to the wavefronts ahead, compressing them. Behind, wavefronts are stretched apart.

simulation_states.label: Key insight — speed unchanged
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Wave speed arrows drawn in both directions — SAME length. Wavelength arrows: shorter ahead, longer behind. Observer A hears higher frequency. Observer B hears lower frequency.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The wave speed in the medium is UNCHANGED. Only the wavelength changes. Compressed λ ahead → higher f'. Stretched λ behind → lower f'.

simulation_states.label: Interactive — adjust source speed
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Slider to change source speed vₛ. Faster source = more compression ahead. At vₛ ≈ v (speed of sound), wavefronts pile up (shock front).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Try increasing vₛ. At vₛ = v, all wavefronts arrive together → sonic boom. The formula f' = f₀(v)/(v-vₛ) diverges when vₛ → v.

trigger_keywords: speed badh jaati hai source aaye toh
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Sound travels faster when the source moves toward you
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: The speed of sound in a medium depends ONLY on the medium properties (temperature, density), not on source or observer motion. What changes is the observed wavelength and frequency, not the wave speed.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show wave speed arrows of identical length in all directions. The wavefronts ahead are closer together (shorter λ, higher f) but they travel at the same speed.
